name: bash-defensive-patterns
description: Master defensive Bash programming techniques for production-grade scripts. Use when writing robust shell scripts, CI/CD pipelines, or system utilities requiring fault tolerance and safety.
capabilities: Generate Bash scripts with strict mode enabled and comprehensive error trapping
Implement safe argument parsing with validation and help text generation
Create structured logging functions with timestamps and log levels
Design idempotent scripts that can be safely rerun without side effects
Build process orchestration with proper signal handling and cleanup
Implement secure temporary file handling with automatic cleanup traps
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 106 static findings are false positives. The skill contains only markdown documentation with code examples demonstrating defensive Bash programming techniques. Command execution patterns, cryptographic references, and file operations are educational examples showing SAFE practices, not executable code. No prompt injection or malicious intent detected.
valueStatement: Prevent common shell scripting failures with defensive programming patterns. This skill provides production-ready templates for error handling, input validation, and safe file operations that protect your automation from edge cases and unexpected failures.
limitations: Requires Bash 4.0 or higher for some advanced features like arrays
Patterns are not compatible with strict POSIX sh environments
Does not execute shell commands directly - only generates code examples
Cannot validate generated scripts without manual testing in target environment

useCases.title: DevOps Pipeline Scripts
useCases.description: Create CI/CD pipeline scripts with proper error handling, logging, and idempotent operations that fail safely and provide clear error messages.

useCases.title: System Administration Utilities
useCases.description: Build maintenance scripts that handle edge cases, validate inputs, and include cleanup handlers to prevent system corruption from automation failures.

useCases.targetUser: Software developer creating build and test automation
useCases.title: Development Tool Scripts
useCases.description: Write developer productivity tools with argument parsing, dry-run support, and comprehensive logging for debugging production issues.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Safe Script
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Bash script that backs up a directory to a backup location. Use strict mode, error trapping, and validate that both directories exist before proceeding.
promptTemplates.scenario: Simple automation task requiring safety and validation

promptTemplates.title: Production-Ready CLI Tool
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a Bash script that accepts input/output file arguments with --input and --output flags. Include argument validation, help text, structured logging, and support dry-run mode.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a reusable command-line tool with proper interface

promptTemplates.title: Process Manager Script
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Bash script that manages background processes with signal handlers for graceful shutdown. Track PIDs, implement cleanup traps, and handle SIGTERM/SIGINT properly.
promptTemplates.scenario: Orchestrating multiple background processes with proper lifecycle management

promptTemplates.title: Idempotent Setup Script
promptTemplates.prompt: Write a Bash setup script that creates directories, config files, and services. Design it to be idempotent - safe to run multiple times without causing duplicate resources or errors.
promptTemplates.scenario: Infrastructure setup that must handle existing state safely

bestPractices: Always enable strict mode with 'set -Eeuo pipefail' at the start of scripts to catch errors early
Quote all variable expansions to prevent word splitting and globbing issues
Use [[ ]] instead of [ ] for conditionals to enable safer pattern matching and prevent unexpected word splitting
antiPatterns: Do not use unquoted variables like 'cp $source $dest' - this causes word splitting on filenames with spaces
Avoid using backticks for command substitution - prefer the modern '$()' syntax for better nesting and readability
Never use 'set -e' alone without understanding pipefail - always use 'set -Eeuo pipefail' together for complete error handling

faq.question: Why does this skill recommend strict mode when it makes scripts exit on errors?
faq.answer: Strict mode prevents small errors from becoming bigger problems later. It stops execution immediately when commands fail, variables are undefined, or pipes break, making debugging much easier and preventing data corruption from continuing with bad state.

faq.question: Can I use these patterns with POSIX sh instead of Bash?
faq.answer: Many patterns like quoting variables and error trapping work in POSIX sh, but some Bash-specific features like [[ ]] conditionals and arrays require Bash 4.0+. For strict POSIX compatibility, use [ ] instead of [[ ]] and avoid arrays.

faq.question: What is the difference between [ ] and [[ ]] in conditionals?
faq.answer: Double brackets [[ ]] are Bash-specific and provide safer handling of special characters, prevent word splitting, and support pattern matching with == and regex with =~. Single brackets [ ] are POSIX-standard but require more quoting and have less intuitive behavior.

faq.question: Why should I use 'command -v' instead of 'which' to check for commands?
faq.answer: The 'command -v' builtin is POSIX-standard, works consistently across all shells, and does not require external processes. The 'which' command is not available on all systems and can produce inconsistent output depending on the operating system.

faq.question: How do I handle script failures that should be non-fatal?
faq.answer: Use '|| true' after commands that may fail legitimately, or wrap commands in if statements to check their exit status explicitly. For more complex logic, you can temporarily disable strict mode in specific blocks using 'set +e' and re-enable it with 'set -e'.

faq.question: What is idempotency and why does it matter for shell scripts?
faq.answer: Idempotency means a script produces the same result whether run once or multiple times. This is critical for reliability - if a script fails midway and is rerun, it should not create duplicate resources, fail on existing items, or leave the system in an inconsistent state.
